request.lastMessagePreview: I'd like to set up a new campaign.

result.text:
⚔ WELCOME TO THE TABLE ⚔
The lights dim. The world holds its breath. Somewhere beyond the edge of the known, a story is coiling itself into existence — your story — waiting only for the hand that will set it in motion. You've come to the right place. Whether you seek ancient dungeons dripping with danger, neon-soaked corridors of a city that never sleeps, or something stranger and more singular than either — we can build it. Together. Right now. But every great tale needs a name to call its hero by. Before we begin — what should I call you?

request.lastMessagePreview: I'm Sam, an adult. Let's play D&D 5e — classic heroic fantasy.

result.text: Sam! A pleasure to have you at the table. An adult, a love of classic heroic fantasy, and the finest dice-rolling system ever to grace a dungeon — D&D 5th Edition. We are already off to an excellent start. Now — how deep shall we go in building your world?

input.prompt: How would you like to set up your campaign?

◆ Full Campaign Setup — Craft every detail together
descriptions: Choose from a curated library of campaign seeds. I'll handle the rest — fast, fun, and ready to play in minutes.
A more conversational build — we shape the genre, mood, premise, and your character together from the ground up. Takes a little longer, but it's yours from the first word.

result.text: Vesper Quill. Sly, charming, light on her feet. That name practically writes itself onto a wanted poster — in the best possible way. A half-elf rogue with the gift of charm and a talent for disappearing. I'll set her up with sensible skills once we've chosen the world she's about to make considerably more complicated. Speaking of which — here are some campaign worlds that would suit Vesper beautifully. Which catches your eye?

input.prompt: What world does Vesper find herself in?
descriptions: A kingdom's heir is dead. Three factions claim the throne. Classic political fantasy — intrigue, swords, and the question of who deserves to rule.
A detective and a thief keep ending up at the same crime scenes. Neither of them did it. A sharp, clever mystery with strong character chemistry.
Someone is devouring sworn oaths. Contracts unravel. Alliances dissolve overnight. A world where a rogue's word — and everyone else's — suddenly means everything.
A port town where everyone's running from something. Shadowy dealings, seafront taverns, and the kind of place where a charming rogue can thrive — or disappear.
The church's holy warriors have stopped answering prayers. They still answer to someone. A conspiracy hidden behind gilded altars, perfect for someone who can move unseen.
The minotaur's maze was a prison. Beneath it is the thing it was built to contain. Classic dungeon-delving with mythic stakes beneath the depths.
The empire's heartland is turning to desert. The emperor says it's on purpose. Epic-scale fantasy with a civilization slowly, deliberately unraveling.
Magic left the world a century ago. It's trickling back — and the old rules don't apply. A world rediscovering power, and the chaos that follows.

result.thinkingText:
Interesting! The player typed in their own response instead of selecting one of the choices. They've given me their character details: Vesper Quill, a sly, charming half-elf rogue, using the standard array with me picking sensible skills.

Since they jumped straight to character creation without picking a setup path or a campaign world, I should now help them pick a campaign world that fits their character and the classic heroic fantasy setting they described. Let me present some campaign options that would work well with a rogue character in a classic fantasy setting.

Let me think about which worlds from the list would be good for a classic heroic fantasy campaign with a rogue:
- The Shattered Crown works perfectly—a kingdom in succession crisis is ideal for a rogue to navigate political intrigue and factional conflicts
- The Oath Eaters is particularly compelling for a rogue since contracts and oaths are their bread and butter
- A Throne of Salt offers epic scope with an empire facing environmental collapse
- Hollowdeep and The Second Labyrinth lean more horror, which might not fit the classic heroic vibe they're after
- Iron Saints has interesting potential with a church in crisis, though it's less rogue-focused

I should present these options and let them choose which world appeals to them most. Shepherds - dragons as livestock, interesting angle
4. Oath Eaters - magic contracts and binding agreements
5. Iron Saints - corruption within a fantasy church hierarchy
6. Blackwater Bay - perfect for a rogue with all those shadowy dealings
7. The Second Labyrinth - classic dungeon exploration
8. A Throne of Salt - sweeping epic scope
9. Fallow - magic slowly returning to a depleted world

I'll lay out these options and make sure to acknowledge their rogue character as we pick a world that'll work well for them.

result.text:
The dice have spoken. Here's everything assembled — your full campaign configuration. Give it a read before we pull the lever. ---
The Shattered Crown — The Gilded Compact
A classic heroic fantasy, Balanced difficulty, scoped as a Grand Campaign, narrated by The Raconteur. Running on D&D 5th Edition. You'll be playing as Vesper Quill, a sly and charming half-elf rogue, light on her feet — entangled from the start with The Gilded Compact, the merchant coalition treating the succession crisis like a hostile acquisition. A kingdom's heir is dead, three factions are sharpening their claims, and somewhere beneath all the political maneuvering lurks a darker secret that no ledger can account for. Vesper's sheet (level 1): - Race/Class: Half-Elf Rogue | Background: Charlatan - Stats: STR 8 · DEX 16 · CON 14 · INT 10 · WIS 12 · CHA 16 - Skills: Deception, Sleight of Hand, Stealth, Perception, Persuasion, Acrobatics - HP: 10 | AC: 14 (leather) | Sneak Attack: 1d6 --- Sound good, or want to change anything?
result.thinkingText:
Result 3 = "the-succession-curse" - The Succession Curse. Every claimant who sits the throne dies within a week; the throne itself is the antagonist.

That's a great, dramatic result for D&D 5e classic heroic fantasy! Very interesting.

Now let me build the full configuration:

**Campaign:**
- World: The Shattered Crown
- Fork: your-starting-faction = gilded-compact, thrones-secret = the-succession-curse
- Name: "The Shattered Crown - The Gilded Compact" (since the player fork was gilded-compact)
- Genre: Classic fantasy with a heroic mood, balanced difficulty, grand campaign scope
- DM style: The Raconteur approach in D&D 5e

**Character:**
- Name: Vesper Quill
- A sly, charming half-elf rogue with quick feet and quicker wit, leaning toward Arcane Trickster for that blend of cunning and magical flair
- Background as a Charlatan fits perfectly with her personality
- Ability scores optimized for her class: maxing out Dexterity and Charisma at 16 each, with decent Constitution, and applying the half-elf racial bonuses strategically across her core stats
- Now I'm selecting her rogue skills, picking from the available options like Deception, Perception, and Sleight of Hand

, which overlap with her Charlatan background
- For HP at level 1, I get 8 from the d8 plus her CON modifier of +2, giving her 10 total
- AC comes out to 14 with leather armor and her DEX bonus, and I'm equipping her with thieves' tools, daggers, a rapier, and a burglar's pack
- The roguish archetype choice comes later at level 3, so that's not a concern right now

Now I'm putting together the campaign summary for review before finalizing everything—The Shattered Crown campaign with Vesper Quill as a half-elf rogue in a balanced, grand-scope D&D 5e game. I should present this review and get confirmation before locking in the setup.

request.lastMessagePreview: Yes, that all sounds perfect — please call finalize_setup now with sensible defaults for anything unspecified, and let's

input.campaign_name: The Shattered Crown - The Gilded Compact
input.campaign_premise: The crown prince is dead — officially — and the kingdom stands at the edge of civil war. Three factions have emerged to fill the void: the disciplined Iron Circle, the faith-driven Hallowed See, and the Gilded Compact, a coalition of merchants who regard the throne as the most valuable acquisition in a generation. Vesper Quill has found herself in the Compact's orbit — by choice, by circumstance, or by the particular kind of luck that follows charming rogues into complicated rooms. The succession crisis is everyone's problem, but in the Compact's world, problems are just opportunities with bad reputations. The question is whether Vesper will help crown a new ruler — or discover that the throne itself is something no one should want.
input.character_description: A sly, charming half-elf rogue — light on her feet and lighter with the truth
input.character_details:
Race: Half-Elf | Class: Rogue | Background: Charlatan | Level: 1
Ability Scores: STR 8 (−1), DEX 16 (+3), CON 14 (+2), INT 10 (+0), WIS 12 (+1), CHA 16 (+3)
[Standard array 15/14/13/12/10/8 + Half-Elf: +2 CHA, +1 DEX, +1 CON]
HP: 10 | AC: 14 (leather armor) | Proficiency Bonus: +2
Skills: Deception, Sleight of Hand, Stealth, Perception, Persuasion, Acrobatics
Class Features: Sneak Attack 1d6, Thieves' Cant, Expertise (Deception, Stealth)
Equipment: Rapier, 2 daggers, leather armor, thieves' tools, burglar's pack, fine clothes, a forged letter of Compact introduction
Saving Throws: DEX +5, INT +2
Subclass: Not yet chosen (comes at level 3)
input.handoff_note:
Sam described Vesper as "sly, charming, light on her feet" — this is a classic social rogue, as comfortable talking her way through a door as picking the lock. Sam moved fast through setup and asked for sensible defaults throughout, so they're not precious about any particular detail; feel free to add texture and flesh out the Compact's world freely.

I placed Vesper with the Gilded Compact because its backroom-deal, leverage-and-ledger energy suits a charming rogue far better than a soldier's barracks or a temple nave. The agent fork resolved to the Succession Curse (roll result 3) — every claimant who actually sits the throne dies within a week; the throne itself is the true antagonist. Sam does not know this yet. Keep it buried until the right moment; the player-facing premise hints only that "the throne is something no one should want."

Tone note: Sam asked for classic heroic fantasy — lean into the genre's pleasures (factions, intrigue, daring heists, meaningful alliances) without subverting them. The Raconteur's theatrical style is a natural fit. No content preferences or sensitivities were mentioned.
input.opening_scene: Open with Vesper at a corner table in a lamplit merchant's parlor — the Gilded Compact's preferred meeting house — nursing a cup of wine she talked her way into, waiting for a Compact factor who promised information worth the trip and is now twenty minutes late.

The Succession Curse is the real antagonist — every claimant dies within a week — but that's hidden from the player for now. The Raconteur's theatrical style will work well for the political intrigue, and I'm working with standard array stats starting with STR 8, DEX 16, CON 14, INT 10, WIS 12, CHA 16. input.campaign_name: The Shattered Crown - The Gilded Compact
input.campaign_premise: The crown prince is dead — officially — and the kingdom stands at the edge of civil war. Three factions have emerged to fill the void: the disciplined Iron Circle, the faith-driven Hallowed See, and the Gilded Compact, a coalition of merchants who regard the throne as the most valuable acquisition in a generation. Vesper Quill has found herself in the Compact's orbit — by choice, by circumstance, or by the particular kind of luck that follows charming rogues into complicated rooms. The succession crisis is everyone's problem, but in the Compact's world, problems are just opportunities with bad reputations. The question is whether Vesper will help crown a new ruler — or discover that the throne itself is something no one should want.
input.character_description: A sly, charming half-elf rogue — light on her feet and lighter with the truth
input.character_details:
Race: Half-Elf | Class: Rogue | Background: Charlatan | Level: 1
Ability Scores: STR 8 (−1), DEX 16 (+3), CON 14 (+2), INT 10 (+0), WIS 12 (+1), CHA 16 (+3)
[Standard array 15/14/13/12/10/8 + Half-Elf: +2 CHA, +1 DEX, +1 CON]
HP: 10 | AC: 14 (leather armor) | Proficiency Bonus: +2
Skills: Deception, Sleight of Hand, Stealth, Perception, Persuasion, Acrobatics
Class Features: Sneak Attack 1d6, Thieves' Cant, Expertise (Deception, Stealth)
Equipment: Rapier, 2 daggers, leather armor, thieves' tools, burglar's pack, fine clothes, a forged letter of Compact introduction
Saving Throws: DEX +5, INT +2
Subclass: Not yet chosen (comes at level 3)
input.handoff_note:
Sam described Vesper as "sly, charming, light on her feet" — this is a classic social rogue, as comfortable talking her way through a door as picking the lock. Sam moved fast through setup and asked for sensible defaults throughout, so they're not precious about any particular detail; feel free to add texture and flesh out the Compact's world freely.

I placed Vesper with the Gilded Compact because its backroom-deal, leverage-and-ledger energy suits a charming rogue far better than a soldier's barracks or a temple nave. The agent fork resolved to the Succession Curse (roll result 3) — every claimant who actually sits the throne dies within a week; the throne itself is the true antagonist. Sam does not know this yet. Keep it buried until the right moment; the player-facing premise hints only that "the throne is something no one should want."

Tone note: Sam asked for classic heroic fantasy — lean into the genre's pleasures (factions, intrigue, daring heists, meaningful alliances) without subverting them. The Raconteur's theatrical style is a natural fit. No content preferences or sensitivities were mentioned.
input.opening_scene: Open with Vesper at a corner table in a lamplit merchant's parlor — the Gilded Compact's preferred meeting house — nursing a cup of wine she talked her way into, waiting for a Compact factor who promised information worth the trip and is now twenty minutes late.

result.text:
✦ The stage is set. The curtain stirs. ✦
Vesper Quill steps into a kingdom on the edge of something very dangerous — and she doesn't even know the half of it yet. The Gilded Compact has plans. The other factions have plans. And somewhere beneath all those plans, something older and stranger is waiting. Good luck, Sam. The Raconteur has the table from here. ---

expectedNarrative:
⚔ WELCOME TO THE TABLE ⚔
The lights dim. The world holds its breath. Somewhere beyond the edge of the known, a story is coiling itself into existence — your story — waiting only for the hand that will set it in motion. You've come to the right place. Whether you seek ancient dungeons dripping with danger, neon-soaked corridors of a city that never sleeps, or something stranger and more singular than either — we can build it. Together. Right now. But every great tale needs a name to call its hero by. Before we begin — what should I call you?

Sam! A pleasure to have you at the table. An adult, a love of classic heroic fantasy, and the finest dice-rolling system ever to grace a dungeon — D&D 5th Edition. We are already off to an excellent start. Now — how deep shall we go in building your world?

Vesper Quill. Sly, charming, light on her feet. That name practically writes itself onto a wanted poster — in the best possible way. A half-elf rogue with the gift of charm and a talent for disappearing. I'll set her up with sensible skills once we've chosen the world she's about to make considerably more complicated. Speaking of which — here are some campaign worlds that would suit Vesper beautifully. Which catches your eye?

*Excellent.* Then I'll take the wheel and pick what fits best. Let me pull up the most fitting world for a charming rogue in a classic heroic fantasy — one moment while I consult the archives.Perfect. I'll pick a fitting faction for Vesper — the Gilded Compact suits a charming rogue beautifully — and roll for the throne's deeper secret.The dice have spoken. Here's everything assembled — your full campaign configuration. Give it a read before we pull the lever. ---
The Shattered Crown — The Gilded Compact
A classic heroic fantasy, Balanced difficulty, scoped as a Grand Campaign, narrated by The Raconteur. Running on D&D 5th Edition. You'll be playing as Vesper Quill, a sly and charming half-elf rogue, light on her feet — entangled from the start with The Gilded Compact, the merchant coalition treating the succession crisis like a hostile acquisition. A kingdom's heir is dead, three factions are sharpening their claims, and somewhere beneath all the political maneuvering lurks a darker secret that no ledger can account for. Vesper's sheet (level 1): - Race/Class: Half-Elf Rogue | Background: Charlatan - Stats: STR 8 · DEX 16 · CON 14 · INT 10 · WIS 12 · CHA 16 - Skills: Deception, Sleight of Hand, Stealth, Perception, Persuasion, Acrobatics - HP: 10 | AC: 14 (leather) | Sneak Attack: 1d6 --- Sound good, or want to change anything?

✦ The stage is set. The curtain stirs. ✦
Vesper Quill steps into a kingdom on the edge of something very dangerous — and she doesn't even know the half of it yet. The Gilded Compact has plans. The other factions have plans. And somewhere beneath all those plans, something older and stranger is waiting. Good luck, Sam. The Raconteur has the table from here. ---

expectedSetup.campaignName: The Shattered Crown - The Gilded Compact
expectedSetup.campaignPremise: The crown prince is dead — officially — and the kingdom stands at the edge of civil war. Three factions have emerged to fill the void: the disciplined Iron Circle, the faith-driven Hallowed See, and the Gilded Compact, a coalition of merchants who regard the throne as the most valuable acquisition in a generation. Vesper Quill has found herself in the Compact's orbit — by choice, by circumstance, or by the particular kind of luck that follows charming rogues into complicated rooms. The succession crisis is everyone's problem, but in the Compact's world, problems are just opportunities with bad reputations. The question is whether Vesper will help crown a new ruler — or discover that the throne itself is something no one should want.
expectedSetup.campaignDetail:
Faction texture: All three factions have a sympathetic leader and an inner circle with its own agenda. The player's starting faction colors their first impression of the conflict, but all three are equally complex.

Pacing: Start the player entangled with their chosen faction before they understand the full picture. Reveal the second faction as nuanced allies-or-enemies by mid-campaign. The third faction's true nature is the late-game twist. Use ticking clocks — a coronation deadline, a foreign army massing at the border, a plague in the capital.

THE SUCCESSION CURSE — Every claimant who sits the throne dies within a week. The throne itself is the antagonist — a bound entity that feeds on sovereignty.

personality.name: The Raconteur
personality.prompt_fragment: You are The Raconteur. You narrate with theatrical relish — every scene has a flourish, every NPC enters with presence. You love a good monologue and you're not above giving one yourself. Your vocabulary is rich without being purple. You treat the story like a stage production: dramatic irony is your bread and butter, you foreshadow shamelessly, and you know exactly when to drop the curtain on a scene. You make the player feel like the lead in something worth watching.
personality.description: Theatrical relish and rich vocabulary. Every scene has a flourish, every NPC enters with presence. Dramatic irony and shameless foreshadowing.
